summary: DHH responds to criticism of Java's generics complexity by arguing that Java's attempt to 'dumb down' programming for less experienced developers has paradoxically made it more complex. He references Joshua Bloch's admission that Java was designed to shield lower-tier developers from Smalltalk-level power. DHH contends that dynamic languages like Ruby offer a better path: more power with less complexity, releasing good ideas from the captivity of over-engineered frameworks.
key_points: Java's strategy of shielding developers from complexity has backfired, creating even more complexity through features like generics
Joshua Bloch admitted Java was intentionally designed for 'lower-tier' developers who couldn't handle Smalltalk's power
Dynamic languages prove that more power can actually lead to less complexity, not more
Ruby on Rails liberates proven patterns and good ideas from Java's unnecessary complexity, making them accessible to all skill levels
money_quotes: Java has become a mace of complexity because of the attempt to shield developers from it.
The realization that more powerful can lead to less complexity is definitely dawning on a lot of people.
It's the release of good ideas from the captive of complexity.
That's how you can get this funny world where the top tiers of the pyramid are enjoying a life without unnatural restrictions and people just entering the pyramid can love the lack of complexity.
How are you going to appeal to that group with a language 'dumped down' for the masses?
key_insight: Attempting to protect developers from complexity by dumbing down a language inevitably creates more complexity, while embracing powerful, expressive languages like Ruby can paradoxically make programming simpler for everyone.
